food_subgroup: Beef dishes — marinated and dressed salad

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bo Tai Chanh (Vietnamese lime-marinated beef salad). FOODON_00002473 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef meat products) is the closest available parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese lime-marinated beef salad in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bo Tai Chanh or Vietnamese beef salad as a specific food entity.

preparation_method: dressed with fish sauce, chilli, garlic, shallots
garnished with herbs and crushed peanuts
main_ingredients: beef (thinly sliced, typically sirloin or eye of round)
allergens: possible gluten (fish sauce brands may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Bo Tai Chanh is approximately 250 g, comprising thinly sliced lime-cured beef, fresh herbs, shallots, crushed peanuts, and dressing, typically served as a starter or light main.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, round, eye of round roast, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; combined with lime juice, fish sauce, and herb garnish component estimates from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Bo Tai Chanh exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for lean cooked beef (eye of round, ~143 kcal/100 g, protein ~26 g, fat ~4 g) adjusted for the lime-marinated salad format with fish sauce dressing, herbs, shallots, and peanuts. The composite dish estimate yields approximately 130–145 kcal/100 g. A conservative mid-range composite of 138 kcal/100 g is used. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — beef and mixed protein-dominant salad dishes; analogous estimate based on lean beef (GI ~0) and low-GI vegetable and herb components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bo Tai Chanh. GI is estimated at 32 based on the dish composition: lean beef has a negligible GI contribution (protein-dominant, GI effectively 0); lime juice, fish sauce, and herbs contribute minimal glycemic load; shallots and small amounts of sugar in dressing contribute a modest GI. The composite GI of approximately 32 (low category) is consistent with analogous protein-dominant Asian salad dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 250 g serving = (32 × 4.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.8, rounded to 1.7 per 100 g equivalent; GL per serving reported as 3.8 but field stores per-serving value of 3.8 — corrected to 3.8 for the 250 g serving. glycemic_load_per_serving field = (32 × 4.8 / 100) × 250 / 100 = 3.84, stored as 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Bo Tai Chanh due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1300 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or a low-sodium dressing is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bo Tai Chanh has a low estimated glycemic index (~32) and is protein-dominant (~18.5 g protein per 100 g), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugary condiments; the modest carbohydrate content (~4.8 g per 100 g) results in a low glycemic load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Bo Tai Chanh provides lean protein and beneficial micronutrients such as iron and zinc, its elevated sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) warrant portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting reduced fish sauce can improve the cardiovascular profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bo Tai Chanh (Bò Tái Chanh) is a Vietnamese dish of thinly sliced raw or lightly lime-cured beef, a preparation analogous to ceviche in which the acidity of fresh lime juice partially denatures the beef proteins, giving the meat a tender, slightly firm texture with a bright, tangy flavour. The dish is dressed with fish sauce, chilli, garlic, and shallots, and garnished with fresh Vietnamese herbs such as Vietnamese coriander (rau răm), mint, and perilla, along with crushed roasted peanuts and thinly sliced onion. The result is a salad that is simultaneously spicy, sour, savoury, and aromatic. In Singapore, Bo Tai Chanh is served at Vietnamese restaurants as a starter or light main course, typically priced as a premium dish. Nutritionally, it is protein-rich (~18.5 g per 100 g) and relatively low in carbohydrates (~4.8 g per 100 g), giving it a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 32. The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) attributable to fish sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is generally suitable due to its low glycemic load and high protein content. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or peanut allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Bo Tai Chanh exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for lean cooked beef adjusted for the composite salad format. GI is an analogous estimate based on dish composition. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the primary ingredient (lean beef) has well-established nutrient data in USDA SR Legacy.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (lean cooked beef as primary component) and USDA Foundation Foods (lime juice, fish sauce, herbs, peanuts as secondary components). No direct laboratory analysis of this dish exists in any approved primary regional database. 2. Sodium content is highly variable depending on the quantity of fish sauce used by individual restaurants; the value of 520 mg per 100 g represents a moderate-use estimate. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this composite dish and is not expected to be a significant contributor. 4. GI is an analogous estimate of 32 (low category) based on the protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate composition of the dish; no direct GI measurement for Bo Tai Chanh was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Vegetables' to 'Meat and alternatives' as beef is the primary macronutrient contributor. 6. Halal status is marked false as traditional fish sauce may contain non-halal processing agents; halal-certified variants exist but cannot be assumed for this record. 7. glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g / 100) × serving_size_g / 100 = (32 × 4.8 / 100) × 250 / 100 = 3.84, rounded to 3.8.
